retrieved_docs.title: Common Variable Immunodeficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: B Cells abnormalities: The number of B cells is normal in the majority of patients, but many of them have reduced percentages of isotype-switched memory B cells capable of producing immunoglobin isotypes that are critical toward antibody response recall. The abnormalities that appear to occur in the bone marrow include aberrant gene rearrangement, decreased V gene replacements, reduced diversity of the naïve B cell repertoire, impaired somatic hypermutation, and abnormal expansion of unmutated B cell clones. [13] Toll-like receptor signaling: B cell maturation is defective, and the action of toll-like receptors 7 and 9 (TLR7 and TLR9) becomes impaired because of a yet unknown mechanism. Defective signaling of TLR7 and TLR9 in B cells and plasmacytoid dendritic cells, along with deficient IFN-a, impairs B cell functions and prevents TLR-mediated augmentation of humoral immunity in vivo. [14] [15] The binding of viral RNA to TLR7 or bacterial DNA to TLR9 leads to B cell activation, cytokine secretion, proliferation, and survival; in CVID, the loss of these stimuli functions may contribute to the B cell defects mentioned. [16] [17] Memory B cells: determining the number of isotype-switched memory B cells is of utmost interest because it provides information about the immaturity of the B cell, relates to the numbers of plasma cells in the bone marrow, and gives information about the possible clinical outcomes. [18] Various studies tried to classify CVID patients based on numbers of isotype-switched memory B cells [19] [20] [21] [22] [23] : Patients that switched CD27+IgM-IgD- memory B cells had a higher prevalence of splenomegaly and autoimmune cytopenias. This proposed a fast and reliable new classification for CVID patients with flow cytometric quantification of class-switched memory and immature B cells. Certain disorders were more prevalent in patients with the lowest count of memory B cells (splenomegaly, lymphoid proliferation, granulomatous disease). Lower levels of IgM memory B cells were associated with chronic pulmonary disease (bronchiectasis). Research has determined that the percentages of switched memory B cells changed in some patients over time. Based on these findings, the suggestion is that immunologic characteristics have to be evaluated several times after diagnosis. [24] Decreased T lymphocyte proliferation to mitogens and antigens, [1] reduced T regulatory cells, [25] T cell receptor excision circles that suggest thymic dysregulation, [26] defective T cell receptor signal transduction. Decrease in CD4+ cell count or increase in CD8+ cell count, altering the CD4/CD8 ratio. [27] Increase in CD26 and CD30 levels (the last ones associated with splenomegaly and malignancy) [28] . The minor capacity of dendritic cells to secrete interleukin-12. [29] A drop of plasma cells in the gastrointestinal tract and bone marrow. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Familial segregation and high concordance rates in identical twins suggest a strong genetic contribution in SLE, although there is no obvious inheritance pattern. Concordant rates for identical twins have been reported as high as 50%. Over 100 gene loci with polymorphisms (or, rarely, copy numbers or mutations) have been identified to be associated with polygenic SLE (majority of cases), and more than 30 genes causing monogenic forms of SLE or SLE-like phenotype have been identified. These genes are associated with activation of the immune system in response to foreign antigens, self-antigen generation, and activation of innate and adaptive immune systems. Some gene mutations that are rare but are considered very high risk for the development of SLE include deficiencies of early complement components C1q, C1r, C1s (>90% risk), C4 (50%), C2 (20%), and TREX1. Some of the other genes associated include HLA-DRB1, HLA-DR2, HLA-DR3, HLA-DRX, TNFAIP3, STAT-4, STAT-1, TLR-7, IRAK1/MECP2, IRF5-TNPO3, ITGAM, etc. The most common genetic predisposition is located at the major histocompatibility (MHC) locus. The MHC contains genes for antigen-presenting molecules (class I human leukocyte antigens [HLA-A, -B, and -C] and class II HLA molecules [HLA-DR, -DQ, and -DP]). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Nephritis -- Etiology -- Genetic Factors
retrieved_docs.content: IFIH1 : This gene encodes melanoma differentiation-associated protein-5 (MDA5)—a sensor for double-stranded ribonucleic acid (dsRNA). This gene variation enhances RNA binding and leads to stronger baseline and ligand-induced type I interferon (IFN) responses. Patients with SLE who carry IFIH1 risk variants exhibit heightened type-I IFN responses and a greater likelihood of developing anti-dsDNA antibodies, which may contribute to the development of lupus nephritis. [10] ITGAM : This gene codes for CD11b-integrin (alpha-M), which is a subunit of the alpha-M beta-2 integrin complex, also known as complement receptor 3 (Mac-1). This protein is expressed in dendritic cells, macrophages, and granulocytes. [8] FCGR : This gene encodes Fc gamma receptors, which have a crucial role in the removal of immune complexes. [8] [9] APOL1 and FcγRIIa : These genetic variants are associated with an increased risk of lupus nephritis in Black individuals. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Both innate and adaptive immune systems play a role in the pathogenesis of SLE. The innate immune system activation is either Toll-like receptor (TLR) dependent or independent. The cell membrane-bound TLRs (TLR 2, 4, 6) are activated on exposure to the extracellular DNA and RNA from dying cells, which leads to downstream activation of the interferon regulatory family (IRF-3), NF-κB, and MAP-kinases, which serve as transcription factors for the production of proinflammatory mediators such as IFN-b. The endosomal TLRs (TLR 7, 9) are activated by single-stranded RNA and demethylated DNA, leading to interferon-alpha production and RNA binding autoantibodies such as antibodies against Ro La, Sm, and RNP. The TLR-independent pathway is activated by intracytoplasmic RNA sensors (RIG-1, MDA-5) and DNA sensors (IFI16, DAI) and leads to activation of IRF3 and NF-κB. Both self DNA/RNA and foreign DNA/RNA, such as from viruses, can lead to this activation. NETosis has recently gained attention in the pathogenesis of SLE. On activation by various factors such as cytokines, activated platelets, and vascular endothelial cells, neutrophils systematically release their nuclear aggregates in the extracellular environment. These nuclear aggregates can then promote Interferon-alpha production by the dendritic cells, mediate thrombosis and vascular damage and serve as self-antigens for T-lymphocytes.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, TREX1 Mutations -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) is a chronic, autoimmune, multisystem, and clinically heterogeneous disorder with a multifactorial etiology in which genetic, hormonal, immunologic, and environmental factors play a role. As with AGS, SLE is notable for its interferon-alpha (IFN-α)  activation signature. Researchers have identified that TREX1 is involved in SLE pathogenesis although rarely TREX1 mutations have been reported in sporadic SLE cases. [29] [31] [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Imiquimod -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Imiquimod is an immune response modifier of the imidazoquinolinamines drug class that induces the production of various cytokines, such as Interferon-alpha, Interferon-gamma, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, interleukin-1, interleukin-6, and interleukin-8. [10] [11] Imiquimod activates the innate and adaptive immune responses via binding to Toll-like receptor 7 and activation of nuclear factor kappa-B with the resultant cytokine release and recruitment of plasmacytoid predendritic cells to the site of medication application with subsequent release of Interferon-alpha and a Th1 response. [12] [13] [14] [15] In addition to its anti-viral activity, imiquimod induces apoptosis of skin cancer cells and has demonstrated anti-tumoral activity. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Discoid Lupus Erythematosus -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: An analysis of 405 patients by Bockle et al. found that smoking is highly associated with discoid lupus erythematosus. Bockle et al. hypothesized that smoking might play a pathogenic role in cutaneous lupus erythematosus variants (DLE, tumid lupus) by inducing apoptosis, stimulating T-cell proliferation, and increasing photosensitivity. Another explanation might be that smoking provokes DNA damage, resulting in the formation of DNA adducts and the production of ds-DNA antibodies. Keratinocytes may also participate in lupus skin damage by increasing the apoptotic rate and the production of proinflammatory cytokines such as IFN-alpha and IL-6 for SLE and IFN-lambda for DLE. [9] [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, TREX1 Mutations -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Previous studies have shown that TREX1 gene deletion in mice leads to inflammatory myocarditis and shortened lifespan due to an interferon-dependent autoimmune response. [7] Recessive missense mutations in TREX1 are mostly associated with AGS, whereas dominant frame-shift mutations are predominantly associated with RVCL. [4] The encephalopathy in AGS and the cardiomyopathy of TREX1 null mice are both autoinflammatory responses. Increased interferon-alpha (IFN-α) levels in AGS and SLE are similar to antiviral immune responses. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon Test -- Etiology and Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: TB remains a significant public health threat worldwide. TB affects all age groups, though it primarily affects adults in middle age. Over 80% of cases and deaths are in low- and middle-income countries. [3] In 2023, an estimated 10.8 million people were infected with TB globally, including 6.0 million men, 3.6 million women, and 1.3 million children. A total of 1.25 million people died from TB, including 161,000 people with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). Tuberculosis (TB) regained its position as the leading cause of death from a single infectious agent after a 3-year period during which COVID-19 was the most fatal infectious disease. TB also remained the leading cause of death among people with HIV and a major contributor to mortality related to antimicrobial resistance. In the same year, the most significant number of new TB cases occurred in the World Health Organization's South-East Asia Region (45%), followed by the African Region (24%) and the Western Pacific Region (17%). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Interferon-Induced Retinopathy -- Pathophysiology -- Potential Risk Factors Leading to the Development of Interferon-Induced Retinopathy
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic hypertension: Retinopathy caused by systemic hypertension can be considered a differential diagnosis for interferon-induced retinopathy. The presence of systemic hypertension may elevate the risk of interferon-induced retinopathy. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Respiratory Syncytial Virus in Adults -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Host immune mechanisms are triggered to produce cytokines and chemokines, including interferon. The cytokines stimulate specific genes, leading to the production of RNase, which possesses antiviral activity. At the site of infection, the RSV protein F triggers activation of toll-like receptors, which initiate an inflammatory cascade of various host innate immune cells, including macrophages, neutrophils, and natural killer cells. Macrophage production of tumor necrosis factor and interferon activates the adaptive immune response.

retrieved_docs.title: Lupus Miliaris Disseminatus Faciei -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Granulomatous variants of rosacea, like LMDF, can present with bilateral, symmetric facial papules with similar morphology. There is considerable histologic overlap, including a relationship to pilosebaceous units. However, LMDF may have larger granulomas that are more prone to caseation necrosis and are not expected to have actinic damage, vascular dilation, or the presence of Demodex mites. [9] The clinical features and course may help discriminate these entities. Notably, LMDF occurs without phymatous changes, ocular involvement, or vascular manifestations of rosacea, such as background erythema, flushing, or telangiectasia. [4] Compared to rosacea, LMDF tends to affect adults at a younger age, including some cases in adolescents and exceptional cases in children; this may affect men more commonly, though other studies note there may not be a gender disparity in LMDF compared with rosacea. [4] [9] The involvement of eyelids, upper lips, and neck is more common in LMDF. Extra-facial involvement can also occur. [6] LMDF is more likely than rosacea to resolve spontaneously within several years but is more prone to significant pitted scarring. As discussed above, LMDF responds somewhat differently to treatment than rosacea, being less successfully treated with antibiotics and more successfully treated with corticosteroids. [5] [6] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anifrolumab  -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Anifrolumab has been shown to correct innate and adaptive immune system defects. Approximately 60% to 80% of adult SLE patients have elevated type-1 IFN inducible gene levels. These patients, especially those with high type-1 IFN gene signature status, had altered protein expression, cytopenias reversed, and normalized immune cell populations when treated with anifrolumab. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1795
retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1492
retrieved_docs.content: Adjuvancia imunologická functions as a category of immunological adjuvants that includes both inosin pranobex and dehydroepiandrosteron. Inosin pranobex is therefore recognized as an immunological adjuvant, while dehydroepiandrosteron likewise serves as an immunological adjuvant and is additionally classified within the ATC group “other sex hormones and modulators of the genital system.” This dual classification of dehydroepiandrosteron highlights its role not only in enhancing immune responses but also as a hormone‑related agent that modulates genital system function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1817
retrieved_docs.content: Prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone are all members of the class of systemic glucocorticoids. Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a group of steroid medications that are administered throughout the body to exert anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects. By being part of this class, each of these agents shares the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoids, including modulation of gene expression, reduction of cytokine production, and suppression of immune cell activity, which are leveraged in the treatment of a wide range of inflammatory and autoimmune conditions. Understanding that prednisone, rimexolone, and paramethasone belong to the systemic glucocorticoid category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy, anticipate similar side‑effect profiles, and apply consistent monitoring strategies across these related drugs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 622
retrieved_docs.content: Deflazacort is classified within the therapeutic category “Immunological Agents [TC]” and is a member of the broader group of systemic glucocorticoids. In the same systemic glucocorticoid class, the agents rimexolone, meprednisone, and paramethason are also included. Together, deflazacort, rimexolone, meprednisone, and paramethason represent systemic glucocorticoid medications that share immunomodulatory properties and are used in clinical contexts where modulation of immune responses is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1858
retrieved_docs.content: Natural and semisynthetic estrogens, plain, and synthetic estrogens, plain are both classified as estrogens, which are sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. These entities represent different sources of estrogenic compounds—natural and semisynthetic versus fully synthetic—yet they share the same functional role in regulating hormonal activity and influencing genital system physiology. Recognizing both categories under the broader class of estrogens helps clinicians understand their similar therapeutic effects and potential side‑effect profiles despite differences in origin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 518
retrieved_docs.content: Mifepristone is classified as a member of the group of progesterone receptor modulators, sex hormones, and modulators of the genital system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1971
retrieved_docs.content: Glucocorticoids, Systemic constitute a class of potent anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive agents, and the drugs prednison, meprednisone and paramethason are members of this systemic glucocorticoid group. As systemic glucocorticoids, these medications share mechanisms of action that involve binding to intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription, and reducing the production of pro‑inflammatory cytokines. Consequently, they are employed in the management of a wide range of inflammatory, autoimmune, and allergic conditions, as well as in certain oncologic and endocrine disorders. Understanding that prednison, meprednisone and paramethason belong to the Glucocorticoids, Systemic class helps clinicians anticipate their therapeutic effects, potential side‑effect profiles, and appropriate dosing strategies across diverse patient populations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2706
retrieved_docs.content: Inosin pranobex is classified as an immunological adjuvant, indicating that it functions to enhance the body’s immune response. This categorization reflects its role in modulating immunity and supporting the body’s defense mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 675
retrieved_docs.content: Synthetic estrogens, plain are classified as estrogens, which are sex hormones and modulators of the genital system. As a synthetic form of these hormones, they function within the same physiological pathways as natural estrogens, influencing reproductive tissue development and function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1368
retrieved_docs.content: Sex hormones and modulators of the genital system constitute a therapeutic class that encompasses both gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants and combination formulations of progestogens and estrogens. Within this class, progestogens and estrogens in combination represent a distinct subgroup, and fixed combinations of progestogens and estrogens are a specific type of that subgroup.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 149
retrieved_docs.content: sex‑hormone framework. Collectively, these relationships delineate a comprehensive map of androgen‑estrogen combinations, multiple progestogen subclasses, their contraceptive and endocrine‑therapy formulations, receptor‑modulating agents, and associated biomarker concepts, providing a structured basis for clinical classification, therapeutic decision‑making, and regulatory categorization.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 42
retrieved_docs.content: combinations” is a subclass of “progestogens and estrogens in combination”, situating fixed‑dose combination therapies within the broader combination group. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical taxonomy of sex‑hormone and genital‑system modulators, linking broad therapeutic classes to more precise formulations and individual active substances such as tibolone and mifepristone, thereby supporting clinicians in understanding drug classification, mechanisms of action, and appropriate therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1256
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydroepiandrosteron (DHEA) is recognized both as an immunological adjuvant (adjuvancia imunologická) and as an anti‑inflammatory steroid. This dual classification underscores DHEA’s capacity to enhance immune responses while simultaneously exerting anti‑inflammatory effects, highlighting its potential therapeutic relevance in conditions where modulation of immunity and inflammation is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 567
retrieved_docs.content: Gonadotropins and other ovulation stimulants are classified as a subset of sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, indicating that these agents belong to the broader category of hormonal substances that regulate and modulate genital function and reproductive processes.
